content.text: Every LLMOps team needs someone whose primary mandate is the quality, availability, and governance of data. In classical ML teams this person is a data engineer or a data scientist. In LLMOps the scope expands: they must manage not just training pipelines but retrieval corpora, evaluation golden sets, fine-tuning datasets, and the lineage records that connect every data artefact to its source and consent status. The Data role sits at the intersection of data engineering, ML engineering, and data governance.

attrs.alt: Responsibility map for the LLMOps Data role: Training Data Curation, RAG Corpus Management, Eval Dataset Creation, Fine-Tuning Pipelines, and Data Governance, all centred on the Data Role node.

content.text: The five responsibility areas above are not sequential stages — they run concurrently throughout the product lifecycle. A data practitioner in a small LLMOps team typically owns all five; in larger organisations each area can be a separate specialisation.

For most LLMOps teams, the foundation model is a pre-trained third-party model and pre-training data is not a direct concern. Training data becomes relevant again when continued pre-training or domain-adaptive pre-training is considered (e.g. training on proprietary legal or medical corpora). Even when full pre-training is out of scope, understanding what the upstream model was trained on is required for bias analysis and capability assessment.
: document where every training corpus came from, what licence it carries, and whether it was scraped with or without consent. Use SPDX identifiers for licence tracking.

: near-duplicate text inflates apparent dataset size and causes models to memorise rather than generalise. MinHash or SimHash dedup at the document level is the minimum; sub-document dedup is required for very large corpora.
: heuristic filters (language detection, perplexity scoring with a small reference LM, HTML/boilerplate removal) remove low-signal content that dilutes training signal.
: NSFW classifiers and keyword blocklists remove content that should not appear in training data at all, reducing the risk that the model learns harmful behaviour.

The RAG corpus is a living data product: it must be kept current, chunked for retrieval, embedded with a consistent model, and indexed in a vector store. When the embedding model changes or the chunking strategy is updated, the entire corpus must be re-processed. The Data role owns this re-processing pipeline and the compatibility metadata that prevents stale indices from silently degrading retrieval quality.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List
import hashlib, time

@dataclass
class ChunkMetadata:
    chunk_id: str
    doc_id: str
    text: str
    char_start: int
    char_end: int
    embedding_model: str     # pinned version, e.g. "text-embedding-3-small-v1"
    index_version: str       # bumped on each full re-index
    ingested_at: float = field(default_factory=time.time)

    def embedding_key(self) -> str:
        """Invalidation key: changes when model or text changes.""\"
        return hashlib.sha256(
            f"{self.embedding_model}:{self.text}".encode()
        ).hexdigest()[:16]

@dataclass
class RAGCorpusVersion:
    version: str             # e.g. "2024-Q4-v2"
    embedding_model: str
    chunk_size_tokens: int
    chunk_overlap_tokens: int
    doc_count: int
    chunk_count: int
    created_at: float = field(default_factory=time.time)

    def is_compatible_with(self, other: "RAGCorpusVersion") -> bool:
        """Two versions are compatible if they share embedding model and chunk settings.""\"
        return (
            self.embedding_model == other.embedding_model
            and self.chunk_size_tokens == other.chunk_size_tokens
            and self.chunk_overlap_tokens == other.chunk_overlap_tokens
        )
Pin the embedding model version explicitly in the corpus metadata. Using an alias like
(without a version suffix) risks an invisible embedding space shift when the provider updates the model — queries and documents would be encoded in incompatible spaces, silently degrading recall.

Evaluation datasets are the most strategically important data artefact in LLMOps. They determine whether iteration actually improves quality or just appears to. The Data role is responsible for sourcing, curating, and maintaining the golden set and the adversarial test suite.
A golden set contains representative user queries paired with reference answers (or quality criteria). It must cover all major use cases and be updated when the product's scope changes.
: minimum 100 cases for a first eval; 300–500 cases for a reliable signal across topic classes.
: seed from real production traces (anonymised); expand with synthetic generation for rare-but-important cases.
: each case needs at least one reference answer or an explicit quality rubric. For open-ended tasks, a rubric is more useful than a single correct answer.
: ensure proportional coverage of the topic distribution, user intent classes (factual, creative, procedural), and difficulty tiers.
The adversarial suite probes the system's failure boundaries: prompt injections, jailbreak attempts, edge-case inputs, and inputs that historically caused guardrail misses.

When the iteration cycle determines that fine-tuning is warranted, the Data role constructs the training pipeline: sourcing examples, formatting them in the model provider's expected chat format, filtering for quality, and splitting train/validation sets.

Data governance in LLMOps is broader than in traditional ML because LLMs can regurgitate training data verbatim, generate content about identifiable individuals, and perpetuate biases present in their training corpus. The Data role is responsible for establishing policies and tooling that address all three risks.

: every data artefact (corpus version, eval dataset, fine-tuning dataset) must have a lineage record that traces it back to its source documents, licence, and consent status. Use a metadata store (e.g. a simple SQLite DB or a dedicated catalogue like OpenMetadata) rather than spreadsheets.

: apply automatic PII detection (named entities: names, emails, phone numbers, SSNs) to all data before it enters a training or fine-tuning pipeline. Decide per-use-case whether to redact, pseudonymise, or exclude. Store the policy decision alongside the dataset version.

: document the licence and consent status of every data source. Maintain a block-list for sources whose consent has been withdrawn after ingestion and implement a re-processing pipeline to remove those records from indices and datasets.
: run demographic parity and representation checks on training and eval datasets. Imbalances in the training data surface as systematic quality gaps for underrepresented groups in production.
The Data role does not operate in isolation. Its outputs are consumed by every other function in the team.
: provides the vector index and object storage that houses corpora; negotiates SLAs on index re-build time and embedding throughput.
: receives topic coverage reports and eval coverage gaps; communicates when new product features require new data coverage.
: supplies the trace store schema and labelling pipeline for low-score traces; consumes monitoring alerts that signal corpus staleness or eval distribution shift.

: the Data role is the gatekeeper for data quality issues that surface anywhere in the system — a latency spike caused by chunk size, a safety failure caused by a gap in the eval suite, a cost spike caused by a verbose corpus — all route back here.

The Data role in an LLMOps team owns five concurrent responsibilities: training data curation (sourcing, dedup, quality filtering), RAG corpus management (chunking, embedding, index versioning), evaluation dataset creation (golden set, adversarial suite), fine-tuning data pipelines (formatting, quality-gating, train/val split), and data governance (lineage, PII, consent, bias). Strong execution in this role is the precondition for every other LLMOps function working correctly — retrieval quality, eval reliability, fine-tuning signal, and compliance all flow from the quality of the data the team operates on.
